Multimeter Mastery.clue: When measuring voltage across a component with a multimeter, this is how the meter is connected.
choices: In series with the component
In parallel with the component
In diagonal with the component
The meter does not need to be connected to the circuit
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: Voltage is measured in parallel. Series connection (correct for current) would not give a valid voltage reading and could disturb the circuit.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: While testing a 24 V DC circuit, this could cause a multimeter to display 235 V.
choices: The circuit has mysteriously multiplied its voltage
Wrong meter range, wrong AC/DC mode, a damaged probe or lead, or induced-voltage 'ghost readings' on a high-impedance meter connected to a floating point
The battery has gained voltage
The meter is working correctly and the reading is accurate
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: A 235 V reading on a 24 V DC circuit is almost always a meter-setup or ghost-voltage issue. The circuit itself cannot spontaneously produce 10× its source voltage.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: Before measuring resistance with a multimeter, this must be ensured.
choices: The circuit is energized and operating
The circuit is de-energized (power off), and ideally the component is isolated from parallel paths
The component is hot
The component is installed in another circuit for reference
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: Resistance measurement requires a de-energized circuit. Energized circuits disrupt the measurement and can damage the meter.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: A multimeter set to the ohms range shows 'OL.' This is what the reading means.
choices: The meter is overloaded and damaged
The resistance exceeds the meter's selected range — most commonly indicating an open circuit (infinite resistance)
The circuit is hot with voltage
The meter is out of calibration
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: 'OL' means 'over-limit' — the resistance is greater than the meter's scale can show. In troubleshooting, this almost always means an open circuit. The meter itself is fine, the circuit is open.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: This multimeter test is the MOST effective way to confirm a suspected broken wire.
choices: AC voltage test
Continuity (or ohms) test with the circuit de-energized
Oscilloscope waveform analysis
Visual inspection only, with no meter
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: Continuity/resistance testing directly confirms whether the wire is connected. AC voltage tests are for energized circuits. Oscilloscopes are overkill for a simple continuity question. Visual inspection alone cannot identify internal conductor breaks with intact insulation.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: If you measure 0 V across a component in an active circuit, this is a possible cause.
choices: The circuit must be powered off
The component is shorted internally, a parallel short bypasses it, or no current is flowing through the circuit due to an upstream break
The meter must be broken
The component is functioning normally
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: 0 V with current flowing means zero resistance (shorted). 0 V with no current flowing can occur when an upstream break eliminates the current, leaving no voltage drop to measure. Either way, it merits investigation.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: This is the primary function of a multimeter.
choices: To generate electrical signals
To measure multiple electrical quantities — primarily voltage, current, and resistance
To convert AC to DC
To amplify weak signals
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: A multimeter measures — it does not generate signals, convert power, or amplify. The three core measurements are voltage, current, and resistance.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: This is what a megohmmeter (megger) is used to test.
choices: Wire length
Insulation resistance between a conductor and ground (or between two conductors), in the megohm range
Signal frequency
Ground bonding resistance in milliohms
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: Meggers test insulation resistance at high voltage. Wire length is measured by TDR or physical measurement. Signal frequency is measured by a frequency counter or oscilloscope. Ground bonding is measured by a milliohmmeter, not a megger.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: This is the primary purpose of a center punch in an avionics repair shop.
choices: To drive nails into hardwood
To create a small indentation in metal that guides a drill bit and prevents it from walking during drilling
To test electrical continuity
To remove burrs from drilled holes
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: A center punch creates a pilot indentation for drilling. Nail driving uses a hammer. Continuity testing uses a meter. Burr removal uses a deburring tool or countersink.

Multimeter Mastery.clue: This is the function of a wire crimper.
choices: To cut wire to length
To compress a terminal or connector barrel onto a wire with controlled geometry and force, creating a gas-tight electrical and mechanical connection
To strip insulation from a wire
To apply solder to a joint
Multimeter Mastery.explanation: A crimper compresses a terminal onto a wire. Cutting is done by cutters. Stripping is done by a stripper. Soldering uses a soldering iron.

Opens, Shorts & Faults.clue: This is why a technician performs a 'wiggle test' near connectors before finalizing an installation.
choices: To add torque to the fastener
To identify intermittent connections — loose pins, cold solder joints, chafed wiring, or cracked bodies — that reveal themselves only when the wires or connectors are moved
To increase the signal voltage
To test the software load
Opens, Shorts & Faults.explanation: The wiggle test reveals intermittent physical connections. It is a diagnostic technique, not a torque application, voltage test, or software check.

Oscilloscope & Waveforms.clue: An oscilloscope is used to visualize this.
choices: Electrical resistance
Electrical signal waveforms (voltage plotted as a function of time)
Magnetic field strength
Pressure
Oscilloscope & Waveforms.explanation: An oscilloscope displays waveforms — voltage vs time. Resistance is measured by ohmmeters; magnetic fields by gaussmeters; pressure by pressure gauges or ADCs.

Precision Hand Tools.clue: When using a manual screwdriver, this is the MOST important factor for preventing damage to the screw head.
choices: Using the longest available screwdriver
Matching the driver tip exactly to the screw head type and size and ensuring full engagement
Using maximum torque regardless of the screw
Lubricating the driver tip with grease
Precision Hand Tools.explanation: Correct tip-to-screw match and full engagement distribute torque evenly. Longest driver is not a factor. Max torque is dangerous. Greasing the driver tip would just make it slip.

Troubleshooting Methodology.clue: This is an important safety precaution when using a megohmmeter (megger).
choices: Leave all avionics connected for a realistic test
Disconnect the circuit from its power source AND from any sensitive solid-state electronics or avionics before applying megger voltage; discharge the capacitance in the system after testing
Apply continuous voltage with no cool-down
Ignore manufacturer maximum test voltages
Troubleshooting Methodology.explanation: Megger voltage damages solid-state electronics and avionics. Isolate before testing. Post-test discharge is important to handle safely. Manufacturer voltage limits must be respected.

Troubleshooting Methodology.clue: This is the primary use of a milliohmmeter.
choices: Measuring high-voltage insulation resistance
Measuring very low resistances in the milliohm range, such as bonding resistance and contact resistance
Generating test signals
Measuring signal frequency
Troubleshooting Methodology.explanation: Milliohmmeters measure very low resistances (milliohms). Insulation resistance is for meggers. Signal generation requires a function generator. Frequency measurement uses a counter or scope.

final.clue: When using a Time Domain Reflectometer (TDR) to shoot a coaxial cable fault, an upward (positive) reflection spike on the trace indicates what type of cable fault?
choices: A direct short to ground
An open circuit or break
Water intrusion
Normal impedance matching
final.explanation: A TDR trace reflects upward for a high-impedance fault (an open/break) and downward for a low-impedance fault (a short).

final.clue: On an oscilloscope capturing an ARINC 429 signal, you observe a square wave that fails to cross the 0V baseline, instead fluctuating between +10V and +5V. What does this indicate?
choices: Normal bipolar return-to-zero operation
A loss of the HI or LO wire, preventing differential measurement
The baud rate is too fast
The termination resistor is missing
final.explanation: ARINC 429 uses a differential bipolar signal (+10V, 0V, -10V). If the scope sees it entirely floating positive, it means one of the legs (A or B) is broken or improperly referenced to ground.

final.clue: When testing a high-voltage generator feeder cable with a Megohmmeter (Megger), why must you discharge the cable immediately after the test?
choices: To reset the meter
To clear the error code
To dissipate the high voltage capacitance stored in the cable
To prevent a false short reading on the next test
final.explanation: A Megger injects high voltage (e.g., 500V or 1000V). A long run of insulated wire acts as a capacitor and can store a dangerous charge that will shock the technician if not discharged to ground.
